section.title: The Wife of Noble Character
section.sectionRationale: The book closes with a complete acrostic poem, each of its twenty-two lines beginning with the successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet, from aleph to tav, forming a full, ordered portrait of wisdom embodied in a working, generous, strong, and God-fearing life. It answers Lady Wisdom's call in chapters 1 to 9 with a sustained picture of wisdom lived out in the ordinary labor of a household.
section.thesis: Wisdom, praised abstractly throughout the book, is here shown fully embodied: tireless in work, generous to the poor, clothed in strength and dignity, wise in speech, and honored above all not for charm or beauty but for fearing the LORD.
section.frameNote: Six clusters follow the Hebrew acrostic's flow: her worth beyond rubies; her hands at tireless work; her generosity and preparedness for hard seasons; her husband's honor and her own strength and dignity; her wisdom and watchful care of her household; her family's praise and the fear of the LORD that outlasts charm.

units.label: Her worth beyond rubies
units.note: the heart of her husband trusts in her

verses.text: A wife of {{chayil|noble character}}, who can find? She is far more precious than rubies.
The heart of her husband trusts in her, and he lacks nothing of value.
She brings him good and not harm all the days of her life.

units.label: Her hands at tireless work
units.note: she rises while it is still night

verses.text: She selects wool and flax and works with eager hands.
She is like the merchant ships, bringing her food from afar.
She rises while it is still night to provide food for her household and portions for her maidservants.
She appraises a field and buys it; from her earnings she plants a vineyard.
She girds herself with strength and shows that her arms are strong.
She sees that her gain is good, and her lamp is not extinguished at night.
She stretches out her {{yad|hands}} to the distaff and grasps the spindle with her fingers.

units.label: Her generosity and preparedness
units.note: she opens her arms to the poor

verses.text: She opens her arms to the poor and reaches out her hands to the needy.
When it snows, she has no fear for her household, for they are all clothed in scarlet.
She makes coverings for her bed; her clothing is fine linen and purple.

units.label: Her husband's honor and her own strength
units.note: she laughs at the days to come

verses.text: Her husband is known at the city gate, where he sits among the elders of the land.
She makes linen garments and sells them; she delivers sashes to the merchants.
Strength and honor are her clothing, and she can laugh at the days to come.

units.label: Her wisdom and watchful care
units.note: faithful instruction is on her tongue

verses.text: She opens her mouth with wisdom, and faithful instruction is on her tongue.
She watches over the affairs of her household and does not eat the bread of idleness.

units.label: Her family's praise
units.note: a woman who fears the LORD is to be praised

verses.text: Her children rise up and call her blessed; her husband praises her as well:
"Many daughters have done noble things, but you surpass them all!"
Charm is deceptive and beauty is fleeting, but a woman who {{yirah|fears}} the LORD is to be praised.
Give her the fruit of her hands, and let her works praise her at the gates.

chayil.gloss: Strength, valor, noble or excellent character, the same word used of mighty warriors and of Ruth, called an eshet chayil in Ruth 3:11. It names capable, valorous strength of character rather than mere social status, framing the whole poem as a portrait of active, strong virtue, not passive refinement.

yad.gloss: Hand, used throughout this poem, verses 13, 16, 19, 20, and 31, to picture tireless, skillful, generous labor. The repetition of hands at work, spinning, planting, giving, forms one of the poem's central threads, tying wisdom directly to diligent, practical action rather than abstract reflection.

yirah.gloss: Fear, reverent awe. Verse 30 sets this fear of the LORD against charm and beauty, both of which fade, as the one quality truly worth praising, closing the whole book of Proverbs on the same note it opened with in 1:7, the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom.

basic.context: The book of Proverbs closes not with another saying but with a sustained poem, twenty-two lines long, one for each letter of the Hebrew alphabet, painting a complete portrait of a woman whose strength, diligence, generosity, and wisdom embody everything the book has praised. She works tirelessly from before dawn, trades shrewdly, gives generously to the poor, speaks with wisdom, and is praised by her whole household, but her deepest praise is reserved for fearing the LORD.
basic.hermeneutics: This acrostic is a poetic portrait, not a checklist, and its structure, moving through the entire Hebrew alphabet, signals completeness, a full picture of wisdom lived out, not a literal daily itinerary any one woman must reproduce. Read this way, the poem celebrates strong, capable, wise living rather than imposing a narrow domestic ideal that would burden or shame women who cannot match every detail.
basic.originalLanguages: Chayil, verse 10, names valorous, capable strength of character, the same word used of mighty soldiers and of Ruth, called an eshet chayil in Ruth 3:11, so the poem's opening line already frames this woman as strong and excellent, not merely decorative. Yad, hand, recurs throughout, verses 13, 16, 19, 20, and 31, binding the whole poem to tireless, skillful, generous action. And yirah, fear of the LORD, verse 30, closes the book on the same note it opened with in 1:7, tying this entire portrait back to reverence for God as the true root of all the wisdom it displays.
basic.typology: This poem answers and completes the personified Lady Wisdom of Proverbs 1 to 9, who called out in the streets inviting all to feast at her table; here wisdom is shown fully at home, embodied in a life of tireless, generous, God-fearing labor. The poem's praise of a life whose works speak for themselves at the gates, verse 31, anticipates Christ's own teaching that wisdom, and faith, are proved by their fruit, Matthew 7:16-20.
basic.christ: This closing poem's vision of wisdom embodied in tireless service, generosity to the poor, and fearless strength anticipates Christ, who is Himself the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:24, and whose own life was marked by exactly this pattern, tireless labor for others, generosity to the needy, and a reverence for the Father that never wavered. And the poem's insistence that charm and beauty fade while the fear of the LORD alone deserves lasting praise, verse 30, points to the deeper, lasting beauty of a life hidden in Christ, whose worth is not cosmetic but rooted in reverence for God, 1 Peter 3:3-4.
basic.god: God is the one this whole portrait ultimately honors, since the woman's crowning quality is not her strength, business skill, or family's praise but that she fears the LORD, verse 30, the very fear the whole book of Proverbs names as wisdom's beginning, 1:7. Every other quality in the poem, diligence, generosity, dignity, wisdom, flows from and returns to that reverence, showing that character rooted in God, not outward achievement alone, is what God finally praises.
basic.crossReferences: Ruth 3:11 calls Ruth an eshet chayil, the same phrase opening this poem in 31:10. 1 Corinthians 1:24 names Christ the wisdom of God, the fuller embodiment this poem anticipates. 1 Peter 3:3-4 echoes verse 30's contrast between fading outward charm and lasting inward worth. Matthew 7:16-20 echoes the poem's closing claim that works praise a life, verse 31.
basic.oneStory: This closing acrostic embodies everything Proverbs has praised, wisdom lived out in tireless work, generosity, strength, and above all the fear of the LORD that outlasts fading charm. The whole book's search for wisdom finds its fullest embodiment not merely in this poem but in Christ, the wisdom of God, whose life of tireless self-giving love is the pattern every reader, man or woman, is ultimately called to reflect.

context.literary: This acrostic poem, 31:10-31, closes the book of Proverbs with twenty-two lines, one for each letter of the Hebrew alphabet in order, a deliberate structural signal of completeness that bookends the personified Woman Wisdom of chapters 1 to 9, moving from wisdom's public call in the streets to wisdom fully embodied within an ordinary household.
context.historical: In an agrarian household economy, a capable wife's labor, spinning, weaving, trading, farming, directly determined a family's material survival and standing, so this poem's detailed praise reflects genuine, weighty economic and social contribution, not merely domestic sentiment.
context.canonical: The phrase eshet chayil, woman of noble character, verse 10, is used of Ruth in Ruth 3:11, linking this closing poem to one of Scripture's most memorable embodiments of loyal, capable, God-fearing womanhood, and the poem's closing emphasis on the fear of the LORD, verse 30, echoes the book's opening motto in 1:7.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To close the book of Proverbs with a complete, celebratory portrait of wisdom fully embodied in daily life, honoring diligence, generosity, strength, and above all the fear of the LORD as what truly deserves lasting praise.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The poem is largely descriptive celebration rather than a list of prescribed daily tasks; its acrostic form, running the entire Hebrew alphabet, signals a complete, idealized portrait of wisdom in action rather than a literal checklist any single woman must reproduce point for point.

debates.question: Is the woman of this poem a literal historical wife, a personification of Wisdom herself, or an idealized literary portrait?

views.label: A literal, historical description of an ideal wife
views.case: The poem's concrete, specific details, buying fields, spinning wool, trading linen, read naturally as a description of an actual capable woman's daily life, meant to commend such a wife as a real, attainable model.

views.label: A personification of Lady Wisdom, bookending the book's opening chapters
views.case: Since Proverbs 1 to 9 personify Wisdom as a woman calling out to be sought, and this poem closes the book with a woman embodying everything wisdom has praised, some read this figure as Wisdom herself finally shown fully at home and at work, completing the book's literary frame.

views.label: An idealized acrostic portrait celebrating wisdom lived out, not a literal checklist for any one woman
views.case: The poem's complete alphabetic structure, aleph to tav, signals a deliberately comprehensive, idealized composite rather than one woman's literal daily schedule; read this way, it celebrates the full range of ways wisdom can be embodied in ordinary life without pressuring any real woman to match every line literally, avoiding a narrow reading that could needlessly burden or shame readers.

debates.bearing: These readings need not be mutually exclusive; the poem may draw on real domestic life while also completing the book's Wisdom personification and functioning as an idealized, celebratory composite. Whichever emphasis is chosen, interpreters should avoid flattening the poem into a rigid, literal checklist that burdens women with an impossible standard; its acrostic form and the whole book's constant refrain suggest the deepest point is that wisdom, embodied in whatever concrete form, is rooted finally in fearing the LORD, verse 30.

study.originalLanguages: Chayil, verse 10, names valorous, capable strength, the same term applied to mighty warriors and to Ruth, eshet chayil, Ruth 3:11, framing this whole portrait as active strength of character rather than passive refinement. Yad, hand, threads through the poem, verses 13, 16, 19, 20, and 31, tying wisdom to visible, diligent, generous labor. And yirah, fear of the LORD, verse 30, closes the entire book on the same note that opened it in 1:7, the fear of the LORD as the true beginning and crown of wisdom.

typology.type: Wisdom fully embodied in daily, generous labor, 31:10-27
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the wisdom of God, whose life was marked by tireless service and generosity, 1 Corinthians 1:24.

typology.type: Fading charm and beauty versus lasting fear of the LORD, 31:30
typology.fulfillment: The imperishable beauty of a life hidden in Christ, 1 Peter 3:3-4.

study.christ: This closing poem's vision of wisdom embodied, tireless labor, generosity to the poor, strength, and wise speech, anticipates Christ, who is the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:24, and whose life displayed exactly this pattern of self-giving service rooted in reverence for the Father. And the poem's climactic claim that charm is deceptive and beauty fleeting, but the fear of the LORD deserves lasting praise, verse 30, points to the deeper, imperishable beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit, of great worth in God's sight, that Peter later commends to believers, 1 Peter 3:3-4, a beauty finally secured only in union with Christ.
study.god: God is the one this entire poem ultimately honors, since the woman's crowning quality, the one thing named worthy of true praise, is that she fears the LORD, verse 30, the same fear the book names as wisdom's very beginning in 1:7. Every other admirable quality in the poem, strength, diligence, generosity, dignity, flows from and returns to that reverence, so that character rooted in God, not achievement or beauty alone, is what finally receives lasting praise.

crossReferences.note: Ruth is called an eshet chayil, the same phrase opening Proverbs 31:10.
Christ is the wisdom of God, the fullest embodiment of what Proverbs 31 pictures.
The imperishable beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit, echoing Proverbs 31:30's contrast of fading charm and lasting worth.
A tree known by its fruit, echoing the poem's closing claim that her works praise her, Proverbs 31:31.

study.oneStory: This closing acrostic completes Proverbs' portrait of wisdom, showing it fully embodied in tireless work, generosity, strength, wise speech, and above all the fear of the LORD that outlasts fading charm. The whole book's search for wisdom comes to rest in Christ, the wisdom of God, whose life of self-giving love is the pattern every reader, man or woman, is ultimately called to reflect.

sermon.title: A Woman Who Fears the Lord Is to Be Praised
sermon.why: Piper's message works directly from Proverbs 31:30 within the setting of the wider poem, Proverbs 31:10-31, drawing out how charm and beauty fade while reverence for the LORD is the one quality that finally deserves lasting praise, a fitting close to this study's emphasis on wisdom embodied and crowned by the fear of God.

quiz.q: What does verse 10 say about a wife of noble character?

options.text: She is far more precious than rubies.
options.note: Her worth is set above even the most valuable material treasure.

options.text: She is difficult to find but not especially valuable.
options.note: The verse explicitly calls her far more precious than rubies, a strong statement of value.

options.text: She is valuable mainly for her physical beauty.
options.note: The poem later explicitly ranks fearing the LORD above beauty, verse 30.

options.text: She is moderately valuable, similar to silver.
options.note: The comparison to rubies, among the most precious ancient gemstones, suggests far greater value than silver.

quiz.q: What activities does the poem highlight in verses 13 to 19?

options.text: Tireless, skillful labor, selecting wool and flax, rising early, buying a field, and spinning with her hands.
options.note: The poem repeatedly emphasizes diligent, capable work.

options.text: Leisure and rest as her primary daily activity.
options.note: This is the opposite of the tireless labor these verses describe.

options.text: Exclusively religious or ceremonial duties.
options.note: These verses focus on practical household and economic labor, not ceremonial duties.

options.text: Managing servants without doing any labor herself.
options.note: The verses specifically describe her own hands at work, not merely delegated management.

quiz.q: What does verse 20 say she does for the poor and needy?

options.text: She opens her arms to the poor and reaches out her hands to the needy.
options.note: Generosity toward the vulnerable is a defining feature of her character.

options.text: She avoids contact with the poor to protect her family's wealth.
options.note: This is the opposite of the generosity described.

options.text: She gives to the poor only through official government channels.
options.note: The verse describes personal, direct generosity, not an institutional process.

options.text: She helps the poor only when it is convenient.
options.note: The verse gives no such qualification about convenience; her generosity is presented as a consistent practice.

quiz.q: According to verse 30, what does this poem ultimately hold up as worthy of praise?

options.text: A woman who fears the LORD, since charm is deceptive and beauty is fleeting.
options.note: Reverence for God, not outward charm or beauty, is the poem's climactic praise.

options.text: Her physical beauty above all else.
options.note: The verse explicitly calls beauty fleeting, not the basis for lasting praise.

options.text: Her business success measured in wealth.
options.note: While her diligence is praised throughout, the explicit climax of verse 30 is fearing the LORD, not wealth.

options.text: Her charm and social skill.
options.note: The verse explicitly calls charm deceptive, the opposite of what deserves lasting praise.

quiz.q: How does verse 31 conclude the poem?

options.text: By calling for her to be given the fruit of her hands, and for her works to praise her at the gates.
options.note: Her own deeds, not merely words about her, are what publicly commend her.

options.text: By instructing her to remain silent about her accomplishments.
options.note: This is the opposite of the public praise the verse calls for.

options.text: By warning that her works will eventually be forgotten.
options.note: The verse calls for her works to praise her, implying lasting recognition, not forgetting.

options.text: By crediting her husband entirely for her accomplishments.
options.note: The verse specifically credits her own hands and works, not attributing them to her husband.

questions: This poem prizes strength, diligence, and generosity as marks of wisdom lived out. What is one concrete way you could embody wisdom more fully in your ordinary daily work this week?
Verse 20 highlights her open hands toward the poor and needy. Who is someone near you right now who could use practical generosity, and what is one specific thing you could give or do?
Verse 25 says she can laugh at the days to come, her strength and honor giving her security about the future. What fear about the future would change if you trusted more deeply in God's care?
Verse 30 says charm and beauty fade but the fear of the LORD is what truly deserves praise. Where are you currently investing more in appearance or charm than in a deepening reverence for God?

The poem closes by letting her own works praise her at the gates, verse 31. What would it look like for your life's quiet, faithful work, rather than your words about yourself, to be the thing that speaks loudest about your character?

translationNotes.note: 'Wife of noble character' translates eshet chayil, literally 'woman of strength or valor,' the same phrase used of Ruth in Ruth 3:11, and elsewhere applied to mighty warriors, framing this figure as strong and capable rather than merely decorative.

This whole section is an acrostic poem in Hebrew, its twenty-two lines beginning successively with each letter of the Hebrew alphabet from aleph to tav, a structural feature necessarily lost in translation but signaling a deliberately complete, idealized portrait.
'Charm is deceptive' can also be rendered 'charm is a lie' or 'grace is deceitful,' a strong Hebrew idiom underscoring how thoroughly outward charm can mislead about a person's true character.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit for Proverbs 31:10-31. Sermon link verified this session via web search of desiringgod.org. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
